author: @stevenbeaumont1698
text: Robert a quick thought crossed my mind when i saw the title of this video. Your conductive ink is it inductive as well . cotton is absorbent old sewing machine bottom of the "N" on the bobbin wind good place  to soak cotton and its cheap allow to soak rinse and repeat with a varnish. better then using copper. . When i watched this video then another popped in my head. Anything with a "E" in its name needs a couple of grills in the front. about the size of the output of a under the desk heater, Good housing and free wheeling Squirle Cage a slight mod let say a couple of magnets + pump cores seperate the coils treat them as individuals all your looking for is a known stable and usalble output from there well posibilities are end less.

author: @davenooner2142
text: I know you were wishing to not use a steel core in your coils. However I urge you to at least try putting a drywall screw or just a little nut in the middle of your coil. You could place them in such a way as to be a bit random instead of just center, stopping any cogging from the magnet attraction. Even a very small washer layed  in the bottom of the center hole of the coil will make soooo much more power and cause little cogging. Try it...you will be surprised! Have fun man!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: The seals are for keeping dirt out which could jam or wear it. Not water. So the permanent magnet rotor actually is in the water and rotates in the water. Not in air. Note that it is symetrical, that is has no out of phase winding to make it go clockwise or not. So when you power it it might go clockwise or anti clockwise. Like the mircowave turntable motor. It makes a great generator demo board.

author: @ianbigland4618
text: I enjoy your prog, but you must rember i n my case you are talking to an idiot child .can you explaine how it works.? bridge rectifiers etc?

replies.author: @dgpreston5593
replies.text:
Hi Ian,
A bridge rectifier changes an alternating current (back-and-forth, like what comes out of the hand crank as the North/South poles of the magnet goes round and round) into a direct (one-way) current that is needed to charge the capacitor and make the LED light to come on.  The rectifier is made from four diodes, that only let electrons flow through them in one direction. The four diodes are connected together in a circuit shape called a "bridge" that does the trick. They come in all sizes, and there is a lot more images and information available online. They are kind of neat, once you see how and what they do.

author: @richardsandwell2285
text:
A brilliant video, I liked the little generator, I will look out for that sort of motor, some hand-crank generators work well when you use an old-style hand drill, it is a kind of Gearbox so it gives more RPM. 
I am so glad you liked the motors, however, they are a mixture of voltages 21V 30V and 230V but they should produce something. 
I have other interesting bits that may be of use, some nice 12volt motors with permanent magnets in them.

author: @herbetone
text: Reminded me of my school days, one of my school chums built something like that or his dad did, except we would all be in a horse shoe shape holding hands with the two end people holding wires whilst he turned the handle and we would all get a small electric shock.....happy dayz......Merry Christmas to one and all.🎄✨🎄 and let us hope 2021 will be kinder to us all.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate and yes let's hope it gets better - all the best
